title: Plans, scheduling and calendars
description: Talk about schedules, priorities, and time management using a range of future and planning expressions.

content.title: Talking about schedules
content.body:
Useful language for discussing schedules and calendars:

Describing what's planned:
â€¢ I have a meeting first thing in the morning.
â€¢ The presentation is scheduled for 2 PM.
â€¢ I've got a packed day tomorrow.
â€¢ My afternoon is free / blocked / taken.

Asking about schedule:
â€¢ Are you free at any point this week?
â€¢ Do you have a window on Thursday?
â€¢ When are you next available?
â€¢ Is your morning free or do you have something?

Priority language:
â€¢ My top priority this week is...
â€¢ The most urgent thing is...
â€¢ After that, I'll focus on...
â€¢ I need to fit this in before Friday.
content.grammar_note:
'Have a window' = have a free time slot
'Packed day' = very busy day with no free time
'First thing' = very early in the morning

content.title: Time management expressions
content.body:
Useful time and planning expressions:

â€¢ I'm tied up until 3 PM. (busy)
â€¢ I'm snowed under this week. (overwhelmed with work)
â€¢ I need to block some time for this. (reserve time)
â€¢ Let me check my calendar and get back to you.
â€¢ I'll pencil you in for Tuesday. (tentative booking)
â€¢ Can we move this to the next slot?
â€¢ I've overbooked myself this week!
â€¢ I like to plan ahead / I tend to leave things to the last minute.
â€¢ Deadline pressure really motivates me.
content.grammar_note:
'Tied up' = busy, unavailable
'Snowed under' = overwhelmed
'Block time' = reserve a specific time for a task
'Pencil in' = put in schedule but not final

vocabulary.meaning: the last time by which something must be done
vocabulary.example: The report deadline is Friday at 5 PM.

vocabulary.meaning: the most important thing to do first
vocabulary.example: My top priority is finishing the presentation.

vocabulary.meaning: a plan for when things will happen
vocabulary.example: Check the schedule and find a free slot.

vocabulary.meaning: the total amount of work to be done
vocabulary.example: My workload is very heavy this month.

vocabulary.meaning: not done by the expected time
to decide how much time or resources to give to something
vocabulary.example: I need to allocate more time to this project.

sentences: I've got a packed day tomorrow â€” back-to-back meetings until 5.
Do you have a window on Friday afternoon?
My top priority this week is the client proposal.
I'm tied up until 3 but free after that.
Let me check my calendar and get back to you.

content.question: Priya says: 'I'm completely snowed under this week.' What does she mean?
options: She is on a winter holiday.
She has nothing to do this week.
She is overwhelmed with work and very busy.
content.correct: She is overwhelmed with work and very busy.
content.explanation: 'Snowed under' = informal expression for being overwhelmed with work.

content.question: Arjun says: 'I'll pencil you in for Thursday.' What does this mean?
options: He will write the plan in pencil only.
He will put you in his schedule tentatively â€” it's not completely confirmed yet.
He will cancel all Thursday plans.
He will call you on Thursday.
content.correct: He will put you in his schedule tentatively â€” it's not completely confirmed yet.
content.explanation: 'Pencil in' = a tentative plan â€” like writing in pencil because it can be erased (changed).

content.question: How do you ask someone if they have a free time slot this week?
options: Do you have a window anywhere this week?
Have you got nothing to do?
content.correct: Do you have a window anywhere this week?
content.explanation: 'Do you have a window?' = professional way to ask if someone has a free slot.

content.question: Which sentence correctly uses 'tied up'?
options: I'm tied up on Wednesday morning â€” I have three meetings back to back.
I tied up my calendar yesterday.
My schedule is tied up empty.
content.correct: I'm tied up on Wednesday morning â€” I have three meetings back to back.
content.explanation: 'Tied up' = unavailable/busy. 'I'm tied up on [day/time]' is the natural structure.

content.instruction: Fill in with the correct scheduling expression from the lesson.

sentences.text: I'll ___ some time for the research this afternoon â€” maybe 2 hours.
My ___ priority this week is completing the budget report.
I'm ___ until 4 PM today â€” can we talk after?
Let me check my ___ and get back to you this afternoon.
I'm ___ under this week but I could make time on Friday.
Do you have a ___ on Thursday afternoon, say around 3?
I'll ___ you in for Friday at 2 PM â€” please confirm by Thursday.
This project is already ___ â€” we need to move quickly.

content.answer: Do you have a window on Thursday morning?
My top priority this week is finishing the report.

content.instruction: Match the scheduling expression to its meaning.

pairs.right: Do you have a free time slot?
I'll add you tentatively to my schedule.
Let me reserve specific time for this task.

content.title: Weekly planning conversation â€” startup office, Bengaluru
content.setting: Kavya (team lead) is planning the week with her colleague Arjun on Monday morning.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Morning Arjun! Crazy week ahead â€” let's do a quick schedule check.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Yes, I already feel snowed under and it's only Monday!

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Ha! So, my top priority this week is the investor deck â€” that's due Friday. What's yours?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Finishing the API integration â€” it's already a day overdue. I need to block at least 6 hours for that.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Absolutely. Do you have a window for a sync with the design team? They need 30 minutes with you.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I'm tied up Tuesday morning â€” back-to-back calls. But I have a window on Wednesday from 11 to 12.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Perfect. I'll pencil you in for Wednesday at 11 with the design team.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: And the client call this Thursday â€” is that still on?

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Yes, 3 PM Thursday is confirmed. I'd suggest preparing your technical summary by Wednesday evening at the latest.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I'll allocate Thursday morning to the prep. I work better first thing anyway.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Good plan. Also, team lunch on Friday â€” that's scheduled for 1 PM at the usual place. It won't take more than an hour.

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: I've got it in my calendar. Looks like a packed week but manageable.

dialogue.speaker: Kavya
dialogue.text: Exactly. Let's check in again on Wednesday to see where we are. Does that work?

dialogue.speaker: Arjun
dialogue.text: Wednesday morning â€” 9:30 AM? That suits me perfectly.

comprehension_questions.question: What is Kavya's top priority for the week?
Why is Arjun tied up on Tuesday morning?
options: He is working on the API.

comprehension_questions.question: When does Arjun plan to prepare his technical summary?

sentences.wrong: I'm very snowed in with work this week.
sentences.correct: I'm very snowed under with work this week.
sentences.explanation: The correct idiom is 'snowed UNDER' (overwhelmed), not 'snowed in' (which means trapped inside due to snow).

sentences.wrong: Do you have a window on Thursday morning free?
sentences.correct: Do you have a window on Thursday morning?
sentences.explanation: 'Do you have a window' already implies a free slot â€” 'free' is redundant.

sentences.wrong: I'll pen you in for Friday.
sentences.correct: I'll pencil you in for Friday.
sentences.explanation: The correct expression is 'PENCIL in' â€” not 'pen in'. Pencil implies it can be changed.

content.scenario: Your manager asks if you can attend an optional training session on Thursday. Your Thursday is already packed with two client calls and a deadline. What do you say?
options: I'm afraid Thursday is completely packed â€” I have two client calls and a project deadline. Is the training available on another day?
Thursday may be okay I suppose.
content.correct: I'm afraid Thursday is completely packed â€” I have two client calls and a project deadline. Is the training available on another day?
content.explanation: Honest, professional response using 'I'm afraid' + clear reason + constructive alternative question.

content.instruction: Describe your ideal productive week. What would your schedule look like? What would be your top priorities? Use the scheduling language from this lesson.
content.prompt: Describe your ideal work or study week â€” what is scheduled, what your priorities are, and how you plan your time.
content.example_response: If I could design my ideal productive week, here's how it would look. My top priority on Monday would be to tackle the most difficult task of the week first thing in the morning â€” when my energy is highest. I'd block two to three hours for deep, focused work before any meetings start. I'd schedule all team meetings on Tuesday and Thursday afternoons â€” that way Monday, Wednesday, and Friday mornings stay free for actual work. I don't like having a packed day with no breathing room, so I'd always leave at least a 30-minute window between tasks. Wednesday would be my creative day â€” I'd allocate time for brainstorming and planning future projects. I'd make sure my Friday afternoon is relatively free so I can wrap up any overdue tasks and plan for the following week. And I absolutely would not leave anything overdue â€” I find that deadline pressure stresses me out, so I prefer to work ahead.

content.instruction: Describe a time when you were completely snowed under with work or study. What was the situation? How did you manage? What would you do differently?
content.prompt: Tell a story about a very hectic, overloaded week using scheduling and time management vocabulary.
content.example_response: Last December, just before our company's year-end deadline, I was completely snowed under. My workload was absolutely unmanageable â€” I had three major reports that were all overdue, a product demo scheduled for a key client, and my manager had added two more tasks to my list without removing anything else. I was tied up from 8 AM to 9 PM every day. I didn't have a single window in my day and I was surviving on coffee. What I should have done differently: I should have blocked time earlier in the month for those reports instead of leaving them to the last minute. I should also have been honest with my manager about my workload â€” I was trying to do everything but doing nothing properly. The lesson I learned was to always allocate time proactively, be transparent about capacity, and never agree to take on more when you're already at maximum. Now I always keep at least one buffer hour in my day â€” I never fully pack my schedule.

content.instruction: Complete Kavya's weekly planning email to her team.
content.text:
Hi team,

Here's a quick overview of our ___ (1) for this week:

MONDAY: Team standup at 9 AM. My ___ (2) priority today is reviewing the client brief â€” it's already ___ (3) by two days.

TUESDAY: I'm completely ___ (4) up in the morning with back-to-back calls. However, I have a ___ (5) between 2 and 3 PM for any quick discussions.

WEDNESDAY: I've ___ (6) out 10 AMâ€“12 PM for the campaign planning session â€” please don't schedule anything in that time.

THURSDAY: Client presentation at 3 PM â€” everyone involved, please ___ (7) this as your top task for the day.

FRIDAY: Team lunch at 1 PM â€” looking forward to it! I'll ___ (8) you all in once I confirm the venue.

Please check your own ___ (9) and let me know if there are any conflicts. It looks like a ___ (10) week â€” let's stay focused!

content.title: Lesson 12 complete â€” Plans, scheduling and calendars
key_points: 'Do you have a window?' = do you have a free slot?
'Block time' = reserve time for a specific task
'Packed day' = very busy day with no free time
